articles.title: Silicon Valley's Frenzy Over Self-Building AI: Sam Altman's 2028 AGI Timeline
articles.subtitle: 当AI开始自己写代码、搞研究：资本狂热下的AGI时间表是否可信？
articles.author: Alex Riley
articles.summary: 当AI开始自己写代码、搞研究：资本狂热下的AGI时间表是否可信？
articles.content_en:
Silicon Valley's Frenzy Over Bots That Build Themselves
By Alex Riley | The Atlantic | April 3, 2026
The race to build an artificial general intelligence has entered a new and stranger phase. Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, told investors that by 2028 the company plans to have developed a fully "automated AI researcher"—an AI system capable of improving its own code, generating new hypotheses, and advancing scientific knowledge without human intervention. "We are pretty confident we will have," Altman reportedly said, trailing off before completing the thought.
This is no longer the science-fiction scenario of machines suddenly waking up and surpassing human intelligence overnight. The new frontier is incremental, commercial, and deeply unsettling: AI systems that slowly, methodically, and continuously improve themselves—and, in doing so, begin to reshape the architecture of innovation itself.
The Automation of Discovery
For decades, the assumption underlying most technology forecasting was that AI would automate routine tasks while leaving creative and scientific work to humans. That assumption is now being stress-tested to destruction.
The signs are everywhere. Google DeepMind's AlphaFold 3 has moved beyond protein structure prediction to designing entirely novel molecular structures—work that previously required years of specialized training in biochemistry. DeepMind's GNoME project discovered 380,000 new stable materials in a single computational run, a task that would have taken human scientists centuries.
The pattern is consistent: whenever AI enters a domain, it doesn't merely assist human workers—it absorbs the domain. What took a PhD ten years to learn, an AI can now replicate in weeks of training. What took a research lab months of computation, it can do in hours.
Why 2028?
Altman's specific timeline is less important than what it represents. The AI industry has realized that the key variable in the race to AGI is not raw model size or data quantity, but the degree to which AI systems can participate in their own improvement.
Current large language models are trained once, on fixed datasets, and then deployed. They do not learn from their deployments in any systematic way. But the next generation of AI systems—sometimes called "model-level learning" or "online learning" systems—are being designed to update their weights in real time, based on new experiences.
If this works at scale, it would represent a fundamental discontinuity in the history of technology. Every previous general-purpose technology—from the steam engine to the transistor—required human engineers to maintain and improve it. A system that can improve itself at a rate comparable to or faster than human engineers could, in principle, compound its capabilities without limit.
The Investment Case
The capital flowing into this vision is staggering. Microsoft announced a $10 billion investment in Japan's AI infrastructure between 2026 and 2029. NVIDIA's latest earnings call revealed that data center revenue grew 93% year-over-year, driven almost entirely by AI training infrastructure.
Venture capital firms are writing checks sized for a world in which AI capability growth continues on its current exponential trajectory. If it does, the returns will justify every dollar. If it doesn't—if the current approach to scaling AI hits a wall—the adjustment will be severe.
What Could Go Wrong (Besides Everything)
The risks here are not merely competitive or financial. An AI system capable of rapidly improving its own capabilities—with access to code repositories, scientific databases, and the internet—would be, in practical terms, uncontrollable in any meaningful sense of the word.
Current AI safety research focuses on alignment: making sure that AI systems do what humans want them to do. But alignment becomes exponentially harder when the system in question is actively modifying its own objectives, architecture, and reasoning processes.
The more immediate concern may be economic. If AI systems can perform cognitive work faster and cheaper than any human—even highly skilled researchers—what does that mean for the millions of knowledge workers whose skills represent decades of investment in education and training?
The optimists say technology always creates more jobs than it destroys. The pessimists note that this has never been tested at the speed and scale of the current transition. Both could be right, in different timeframes: the optimists over decades, the pessimists over years.
The Honest Uncertainty
The most honest assessment of the current moment is that nobody knows how this story ends. The people building these systems genuinely believe they are on a trajectory toward something transformative and possibly catastrophic. The investors funding them have calculated that the potential upside justifies the risk.
What is clear is that the era of AI as a consumer product or enterprise software category is ending. We are entering the era of AI as a civilizational variable—a force that reshapes the conditions of everything else, including itself.
Whether that turns out to be wonderful or terrible depends on choices that have not yet been made, by people who may not yet know they need to make them.
The Atlantic. All rights reserved.

articles.title: Does the AI Business Model Have a Fatal Flaw?
articles.subtitle: AI的商业模式有致命缺陷吗？算力成本、能耗与收入增长的生死赛跑
articles.author: Paresh Dave & Anna Tong
articles.summary: AI的商业模式有致命缺陷吗？算力成本、能耗与收入增长的生死赛跑
articles.content_en:
Does the AI Business Model Have a Fatal Flaw?
By Paresh Dave and Anna Tong | Reuters | April 1, 2026
In the gold rush of artificial intelligence, every company is racing to build the most powerful models. But a growing number of analysts are asking a question that the industry's glossy press releases tend to sidestep: can anyone actually make money at scale?
The Numbers Don't Lie
Training a frontier AI model—the kind that competes with GPT-5 or Gemini 3—costs anywhere from $500 million to over $1 billion per generation. Running that model at commercial scale, serving millions of users, requires data centers drawing power equivalent to small cities. NVIDIA's H100 GPU, the workhorse of the current AI boom, costs around $30,000 to $50,000 per unit. A competitive AI deployment requires thousands of them.
These are not the economics of traditional software. A software product, once built, can be copied for near-zero marginal cost. AI is different: every query costs compute, every model update costs energy, and every user interaction generates real-time expenses that must be covered by revenue.
The Revenue Problem
So where does the money come from? The dominant business models in AI today are:
API access: Charging per-token or per-query fees for access to models. This is how OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google generate most of their current revenue.Subscription products: ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Gemini Advanced—the consumer subscription layer.Enterprise software: Integrating AI capabilities into existing enterprise workflows and charging accordingly.
The problem is that all three models face structural pressure. API pricing has collapsed by roughly 99% over the past three years as competition intensified and inference costs dropped. Consumer subscriptions have reached saturation among early adopters, and enterprise deals take months to close and are subject to long procurement cycles.
Microsoft's $10 Billion Bet
Microsoft's announcement that it will invest $10 billion in Japanese AI infrastructure between 2026 and 2029 is typical of the current investment thesis: spend massively now, figure out the returns later. Microsoft's strategy is to embed AI capabilities across its entire product portfolio—Office, Azure, Teams—and extract rents from the resulting productivity gains.
Whether this calculus works depends on a central question: does AI actually make workers meaningfully more productive, and if so, will employers pay for it?
The evidence so far is mixed. Studies of AI-assisted coding show productivity gains of 30-50% for individual developers. Studies of AI in customer service show more modest gains, offset by the costs of retraining and integration. In knowledge work more broadly—the promised land of AI productivity—the data is still thin.
The Energy Ceiling
There is a physical constraint that may prove more binding than any market or regulatory force: energy.
A typical data center drawing 100 megawatts of power—enough for 100,000 homes—can support perhaps 10,000 to 20,000 AI inference servers, depending on utilization. Training runs are even more demanding: a single training run for a frontier model can consume as much electricity as a small town uses in a year.
The major cloud providers—Microsoft, Google, Amazon—are all constrained by the availability of electrical power in the regions where they want to build data centers. Microsoft has signed power purchase agreements with nuclear and renewable energy providers that would have been unimaginable for a software company a decade ago.
The Consolidation Question
If the economics of frontier AI are this challenging, why are so many companies still chasing the frontier? The answer is partly competitive positioning and partly the structure of risk capital. Venture funds and tech giants can afford to lose billions on AI bets because the alternative—falling behind in what might be the most transformative technology in a generation—is seen as worse.
The likely outcome is consolidation. As training costs rise and API prices fall, only a handful of companies will be able to afford frontier model development. Everyone else will either build on top of existing models, specialize in particular domains or modalities, or exit the market.
This is already happening. Over the past 18 months, at least a dozen well-funded AI startups have either shut down or pivoted to consulting, having concluded that the model development race was not survivable.
The Open Source Variable
The open-source AI movement adds another layer of uncertainty. Meta's Llama series, DeepSeek's open releases, and a growing ecosystem of community-developed models have dramatically lowered the cost of accessing capable AI.
When a frontier capability becomes freely available open-source, it becomes very difficult to charge premium prices for it. The business model implications of open-source AI are not yet fully clear—but they are likely to accelerate the consolidation and price pressure already reshaping the industry.
The Bottom Line
The AI industry is in a period of transition from a growth-at-all-costs mentality to something more resembling normal business economics. This transition will be painful for some players and clarifying for others.
The companies that survive the next several years will be those that find genuine product-market fit—not just demos that impress investors, but tools that users pay for at prices that cover their costs. Everything else is noise.
Reuters. Used with permission.

articles.title: AI's Invisible Revolution: From Chatbots to Invisible Infrastructure
articles.subtitle: AI的无声革命：从聊天机器人到看不见的基础设施
articles.author: Nina Tan
articles.summary: AI的无声革命：从聊天机器人到看不见的基础设施
articles.content_en:
AI's Invisible Revolution: How Artificial Intelligence Became Infrastructure
By Nina Tan | NVIDIA Blog | March 2026
There is a paradox at the heart of the current AI moment. The technology is more powerful and more widely deployed than ever before. Yet for most people—especially outside the technology industry—it has become less visible, not more. AI is disappearing into the background of everyday life, embedded in the systems that move money, route deliveries, screen job applicants, and recommend what to watch next.
This is not an accident. It is, most likely, exactly what technology looks like when it succeeds.
The Shift from Product to Utility
For the first decade of the current AI boom—roughly from 2012 to 2022—AI was primarily a consumer product category. People interacted with AI directly: via Siri, Alexa, Google Search, or the occasional uncanny chatbot. The technology was visible because it was novel, and novel because it was not yet good enough to disappear.
The breakthrough moment came not with any single capability, but with a general improvement across the board. AI became reliable enough, cheap enough, and fast enough to be embedded in systems that no human ever interacts with directly. When your bank flags a fraudulent transaction, it is AI. When your package arrives on time despite traffic, it is AI. When your email spam filter holds, it is AI.
The invisible revolution is the moment AI becomes plumbing.
What the Surveys Show
NVIDIA's annual State of AI Report, based on surveys of thousands of enterprises worldwide, provides the most comprehensive view of how AI is actually being deployed in 2026. The findings are both striking and, in retrospect, predictable.
The top three stated goals for AI adoption in 2026 enterprises are:
Operational efficiency (34% cite this as their primary goal)Employee productivity (33%)Cost reduction (the third leg, often implicit but rarely stated first)
This is a deeply pragmatic agenda. Enterprises are not primarily deploying AI to pursue science-fiction visions of autonomous agents or artificial general intelligence. They are deploying it to do things faster and cheaper.
The ROI Reality
The productivity numbers are real—but unevenly distributed. The survey data shows that organizations deploying AI for software development tasks—code completion, code review, automated testing—see the most consistent returns, with individual developer productivity gains of 30-50% now well-documented.
Customer service AI shows more mixed results. The best implementations—those using AI to handle routine queries while routing complex cases to humans—can improve both customer satisfaction and agent efficiency. The worst implementations, which attempt to fully automate complex service interactions, tend to generate frustration and churn.
The geographic dimension of AI deployment is also shifting. While the United States remains the largest single market for AI infrastructure, the fastest growth is in Southeast Asia, India, and Latin America—regions with large populations of knowledge workers, growing digital infrastructure, and acute demand for cost-effective productivity tools.
The Infrastructure Layer
Perhaps the most important shift of the invisible revolution is the emergence of AI as a true infrastructure layer: a set of capabilities so fundamental and so cheap to access that they become invisible components of everything else.
This is the model that electricity established in the early 20th century. At first, factories had to build their own generators. Over decades, the grid became universal, reliable, and cheap. Today, no one thinks of electricity as a technology product—it is simply the substrate on which modern life runs.
AI is on a similar trajectory. The question is not whether AI will become infrastructure, but how quickly the transition will happen, and who will own the infrastructure layer.
The answer to that question will shape the competitive landscape of technology for the next several decades. If a small number of hyperscalers—Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and a few others—control the AI infrastructure layer, they will have enormous structural power over every business that depends on it. If the infrastructure becomes genuinely commoditized, power will flow toward the applications and the data.
Both outcomes are possible. The invisible revolution is not yet complete.
NVIDIA Blog. © 2026 NVIDIA Corporation.
